content.full_text:
September 22, 1976


Mr. Philip G. Satre
Vargas, Bartlett and Dixon
P. O. Box 281
Reno, Nevada

Dear Mr. Satre:

In response to your letter of September 7, confirming advice
given to you by telephone on August 30, 1976, the information
you outline is correct.

The value of complimentary food, lodging, etc., extended to an
elected state official other than in connection with direct
communication or any other activity in an effort to influence
legislative or administrative action, are gifts to the legislator
and must be reported on his or her Statement of Economic Interests
report filed annually if they have an aggregated value of $25 or
more. The extension of the aforementioned "gift" would not be
involved in a determination as to whether or not an entity has a
requirement to file under Sections 86108(b) or 86109 of the
Government Code.

Very truly yours,


REGINA SICILIANO-KUTCHINS
Technical Assistance Program Coordinator

RSK:mak

RENO OFFICE                                                                                                    LAS VEGAS OFFICE
GEORGE L.VARGAS                          VARGAS, BARTLETT & DIXON                                    300 SOUTH FOURTH STREET
KENNETH P. DILLON (1914-1964)                    ATTORNEYS AT LAW                                              800 VALLEY BANK PLAZA
WILLIAM BARTLETT                         201 WEST LIBERTY STREET, SUITE 300                          LAS VEGAS, NEVADA 89101
LOUIS MEAD DIXON                                                                                                   (702) 384-8703
ROBERT N. MARSHALL                            POST OFFICE BOX 281
JAMES P. LOGAN                            
JOHN C. RENSHAW                              RENO, NEVADA 89504                                       LOUIS MEAD DIXON
JAMES W. DIXON                                                                                                   ROBERT L.GIFFORD
FREDERIC R. STARICH                            (702) 785-5000                                                  J. GREGORY NASH
MARVIN W. MURPHY                                                                                                 EDWARD H. THICKER
JAMES S. BEASLEY                              CABLE: VARSADIX                                         M. DOUGLAS STEWART
JOHN P. SANDS, III                                                                                              CHRIS A. BECCROFT, JR.
JOSEPH M. DINI, JR.                                                                                             DEAN R. VERNON
PHILIP G. SATRE
JAMES P. LOGAN, JR.
                                              September 7, 1976


Regina Kutchins
Technical Compliance Advisor
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Dear Ms. Kutchins:

     I would like, through this letter, to confirm my telephone conversations with you of Monday, August 30, 1976, and Wednesday, September 1, 1976.  In the referenced conversations I posed to you the hypothetical question of whether or not an extension of complimentary food, lodging, etc., by a hotel-restaurant to any elective state official, legislative official or agency official or a member of the immediate family of any such official would constitute a payment to influence legislative or administrative action under Government Code §82045 (thereby requiring inclusion in the sums aggregated to determine whether a person must file as a "$250" filer under §86108(b)).

     Your answer was, I believe, as follows:  The value of the complimentary food, lodging, etc. is not aggregated toward the $250 unless the hotel-restaurant is "lobbying" the person to whom the benefits are extended.  You further stated that the recipient of the benefits would have to disclose all benefits exceeding $25.00 in value on his or her conflict of interest statement.

     It is my understanding, based upon our conversation, that in the following situations there would be no reporting under §§86108(b) and 86109:

     (1)  The hotel-restaurant extends complimentary benefits to an elective state official, etc., with whom the hotel-restaurant has had no contact whatsoever.

Regina Kutchins
September 7, 1976
Page Two


(2) The hotel-restaurant extends complimentary
benefits to an elective state official, etc., whom it has
had contact with on official matters in the past (or
possibly in the future) but with whom it has no contact
during the month preceding or succeeding the date of the
extension of the complimentary benefits.

(3) The hotel-restaurant extends complimentary benefits
to an elective state official, etc., in the ordinary course of
business (i.e., as organizer of a convention [non-campaign] where
it is the hotel's practice to comp the organizer).

I further understand that the value of the complimentary
benefits must be aggregated for filing purposes under §§86108(b)
and 86109 where the recipient is, at the time of the extension of
such benefits, an elective state official, etc., who is being
contacted in his official capacity by the person extending the
benefits.

If the foregoing accurately reflects your understand-
ing, would you please write me a confirming letter rather than
a formal opinion under Government Code §83114.

Thank you for your kind cooperation and assistance in
this matter.

Yours truly,

[signature]

Philip G. Satre

PGS/mgg

sections.question: Whether the extension of complimentary food, lodging, etc., by a hotel-restaurant to any elective state official, legislative official or agency official or a member of the immediate family of any such official would constitute a payment to influence legislative or administrative action under Government Code §82045 (thereby requiring inclusion in the sums aggregated to determine whether a person must file as a "$250" filer under §§86100 et seq.), and the reporting requirements for recipients of such benefits.
sections.conclusion: The value of complimentary food, lodging, etc., extended to an elected state official other than in connection with direct communication or any other activity in an effort to influence legislative or administrative action, are gifts to the legislator and must be reported on his or her Statement of Economic Interests report filed annually if they have an aggregated value of $25 or greater. The extension of the aforementioned "gift" would not be involved in a determination as to whether or not an entity has a requirement to file under Sections 86100 or 86109 of the Government Code.
sections.question_synthetic: Does providing complimentary food and lodging to elected officials constitute a reportable gift or payment intended to influence legislative action, and when must recipients disclose such benefits on conflict of interest statements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Complimentary benefits provided to elected officials must be reported as gifts if they exceed $25 in value on the recipient's economic interest statement, but do not constitute lobbying payments requiring the provider to file as a lobbyist unless the provider is directly attempting to influence legislative action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter (dated September 7, 1976) and the FPPC response letter (dated September 22, 1975 - appears to be a date error in original document). Quality of OCR scan is poor with significant character recognition errors throughout. Extracted conclusion from FPPC response section. Incoming request provides detailed hypothetical scenarios which inform the question_synthetic.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the extension of complimentary food, lodging, etc., by a hotel-restaurant to any elective state official, legislative official or agency official or a member of the immediate family of any such official would constitute a payment to influence legislative or administrative action under Government Code §82045 (thereby requiring inclusion in the sums aggregated to determine whether a person must file as a "$250" filer under §§86100 et seq.), and the reporting requirements for recipients of such benefits.

CONCLUSION: The value of complimentary food, lodging, etc., extended to an elected state official other than in connection with direct communication or any other activity in an effort to influence legislative or administrative action, are gifts to the legislator and must be reported on his or her Statement of Economic Interests report filed annually if they have an aggregated value of $25 or greater. The extension of the aforementioned "gift" would not be involved in a determination as to whether or not an entity has a requirement to file under Sections 86100 or 86109 of the Government Code.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to inquiry about whether complimentary food and lodging provided by a hotel-restaurant to elected officials constitute reportable gifts or lobbying payments, clarifying that such benefits must be disclosed by recipients if they exceed $25 but do not trigger lobbying registration unless extended as part of a direct effort to influence legislative action.
